name: Tyree Glenn
abstract: Tyree Glenn, born William Tyree Glenn (November 23, 1912, Corsicana, Texas, United States, – May 18, 1974, Englewood, New Jersey), was an American trombone and vibraphone player.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Dody Goodman in "Dody in Dixieland"

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: So among them, among the older musicians was Don Bias and Quinton Jackson on trombone, Tyree Glenn or trombone and vibraphone... So we went on this tour... We traveled a lot by bus so we got to know one another very well... Don Bias and Tyree Glenn then took the band to Holland and we played in Rott
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jo Jones
about_quotes.evidence: It was a Jo Jones trio featuring Tyree Glenn. We came in for one week and we stayed 17 weeks. Then the next time we came back in for 16 weeks, it was Tyree Glenn's quartet with the same people

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: They had a quartet used to go out front. Chu Berry, Tyree Glenn, Cozy Cole, myself, and Hinton. Five. A quintet. They called it Cab Jivers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Danny Barker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: we had a little thing they called them the Cab Jivers, we used to go down front, play, Tyree Glenn, Milt Hinton, me, and Chu.

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Well the only significant engagement was at The Roundtable with Tyree Glen. It was a quartet... I learned a lot with Tyre.
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: We was very close, very, very close. People don't know, really, how close we were, you know.
